topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into a perfectly mundane Tuesday afternoon. My thumb hovered over the link, a knot tightening in my chest. It was an image of the old oak tree in the front yard, the exact shade of green I remembered from scraped knees and summer afternoons. Below it, the address: my childhood home. A cold, sharp feeling washed over me, making my jaw clench. I clicked. The listing details swam before my eyes—a price tag, photos of peeling paint, the unsettling familiarity of the layout. I reread the description, a slow, deliberate motion, my breath catching in shallow gasps. "Are you serious?" I muttered, the sound rough in my throat. I slammed my laptop shut, the plastic protesting the sudden force. I stood up, pacing the small living room, my hands shaking slightly as I ran them through my hair. This wasn't just a house; it was a memory, and someone was trying to erase it.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. My knuckles were white where I gripped the mouse. This was impossible. The address scrolled by, and then, there it was—the familiar crooked porch swing, the faded blue shutters. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I could feel the tension radiating off me like heat. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a long, shaky exhale that sounded more like a frustrated snarl. Why? Who had the audacity to put *this* on the market? I started furiously refreshing the page, checking the details, looking for some ridiculous mistake, some glitch in the system. Every detail confirmed the horror: the address was mine. My mind raced, cycling through possibilities—a prank, a mistake, a scam—but the sheer weight of the reality pressed down on me. I pushed myself away from the desk, needing to move, needing to do something other than just sit there and absorb the sheer unfairness of it all.

[story 3]

When the email landed, I didn't even bother reading the boilerplate pleasantries. My eyes locked onto the property details, and a low growl started deep in my stomach. It was a visceral reaction, a tightening in my chest that made breathing shallow. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to smash the keyboard. I typed a quick search into the search bar, trying to find a retraction, an error message, anything to explain this grotesque intrusion. The photos—the worn welcome mat, the chipped paint on the window frame—they mocked me. I stood up abruptly, my chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. I walked to the window, peering out at the street, feeling a profound sense of violation. This place was sacred ground, and now it was being auctioned off to strangers. I clenched my fists so hard my nails dug into my palms, the anger building into a tight, pressurized core behind my eyes. I needed to know who was doing this, and I needed to find them.
